plainName: Someone Tries Many Passwords to Get In
realWorldScenario: An attacker uses a list of stolen passwords or automatically tries millions of password combinations until they guess yours. It's like someone trying every key on a keyring against your lock.
targetedTypes: All businesses and individuals, especially banks and email users
howYouWouldKnow: Strange logins from places you've never been, account locked after many failed attempts, emails sent from your account to unknown people
singleActionToTake: Turn on Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) immediately on all important accounts - this blocks attackers even if they guess your password
industryNotes: Banks and finance companies are targeted most often with this attack

plainName: Fake Emails That Trick You Into Clicking
realWorldScenario: Attacker sends you an email that looks like it's from someone you trust (your bank, a colleague, a vendor) but it's actually from them. The email tricks you into clicking a link or opening an attachment that installs malware.
targetedTypes: Everyone - it's the #1 attack method against all organizations
howYouWouldKnow: Email from someone asking unusual things, links to websites that ask for your password, attachments from unexpected senders, unusual grammar or spelling
singleActionToTake: Be very suspicious of emails asking you to click links or download files - when in doubt, contact the sender directly by phone or separate email
industryNotes: Used by 95% of attackers - it works because humans are trusting

plainName: Malware That Locks Your Files For Ransom
realWorldScenario: Attacker's malware encrypts (locks) all your important files - documents, photos, databases - so you can't use them. Then they demand payment to unlock them. This is called ransomware.
dangerLevel: Highly Difficult (but catastrophic when it happens)
targetedTypes: Healthcare organizations, businesses with valuable data, anyone with important files
howYouWouldKnow: Files can't be opened, you see a message demanding payment, computer slows down, files have strange extensions like .locked or .encrypted
singleActionToTake: Back up all important files DAILY to a separate external hard drive or cloud storage that's not connected to your main computer
industryNotes: Ransomware attacks on healthcare cost average $500,000+ per organization

plainName: Fake Emails with Dangerous Attachments
realWorldScenario: Attacker sends an email with an attachment (like a PDF, Word document, or Excel spreadsheet) that looks innocent but contains malicious code. When you open it, the malware installs on your computer.
targetedTypes: Office workers, anyone who receives email attachments
howYouWouldKnow: Unexpected attachment from someone, file from unknown sender, antivirus warning when opening attachment
singleActionToTake: Never open attachments from unknown senders. If it's from someone you know but unexpected, contact them first to confirm

plainName: Fake Emails with Dangerous Links
realWorldScenario: Attacker sends an email with a link that looks safe but actually takes you to a fake website that steals your password, or a website that installs malware on your computer.
targetedTypes: Anyone who clicks links in emails
howYouWouldKnow: Link in email doesn't match where it says it goes, landing page asks for password, website looks slightly different than the real one
singleActionToTake: Hover over links before clicking to see the real URL, or manually type the website address instead of clicking the link

plainName: Attacker Uses Stolen or Default Passwords
realWorldScenario: Attacker has obtained legitimate usernames and passwords (from a data breach, from phishing, or because the password is still the default). They use these real credentials to log in as a real user.
targetedTypes: Any organization with user accounts
howYouWouldKnow: Account activity from unusual locations, password changes you didn't make, unusual file access or data downloads
singleActionToTake: Change all passwords immediately, use unique strong passwords for each account, enable password managers

plainName: Attacker Adds Themselves to Your Accounts
realWorldScenario: Attacker gets access to your IT systems and adds their own user account or email address to existing accounts, giving them permanent access even if you change the original password.
targetedTypes: Medium to large organizations with multiple user accounts
howYouWouldKnow: Unexpected user accounts in your system, email forwarding rules you didn't create, new devices added to your cloud account
singleActionToTake: Regularly review all user accounts and permissions in your systems - remove any you don't recognize

plainName: Attacker Searches for Your Information Online
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses Google, LinkedIn, company websites, and public databases to find information about your organization - employee names, email patterns, phone numbers, software you use. This information is used for more targeted attacks later.
targetedTypes: Any organization with a web presence
howYouWouldKnow: You won't know this is happening - it's passive research
singleActionToTake: Review your company's public information - remove unnecessary details from websites, ask IT to limit what appears in search results

plainName: Attacker Steals Data From Your Computer or Network
realWorldScenario: Attacker gains access to your computer or network and copies files, databases, or other valuable information to steal it. This could be customer data, financial records, intellectual property, or personal information.
targetedTypes: Organizations with valuable data (banks, tech companies, retailers with customer data)
howYouWouldKnow: Large amounts of data transferred to unusual locations, network congestion, missing files or data
singleActionToTake: Encrypt sensitive files, limit access to data, monitor who accesses what files, back up everything regularly
industryNotes: Data breaches cost companies $4.29 million on average

plainName: Attacker Creates New Fake User Accounts
realWorldScenario: Attacker creates new user accounts in your system to maintain long-term access. These fake accounts look like legitimate employee accounts but are controlled by the attacker.
targetedTypes: Medium to large organizations with Active Directory or user management systems
howYouWouldKnow: New user accounts you don't recognize, accounts with unusual names, accounts with admin privileges that shouldn't have them
singleActionToTake: Regularly audit all user accounts - review monthly who has access to what and remove unnecessary accounts

plainName: Attacker Installs Malware That Starts Automatically
realWorldScenario: Attacker installs malware that automatically starts every time the computer boots up. This gives the attacker persistent access - even if you restart your computer, the malware is still running.
targetedTypes: Any organization vulnerable to malware
howYouWouldKnow: Computer slower than usual, unexpected processes running, antivirus alerts, disk activity even when you're not using the computer
singleActionToTake: Keep antivirus and malware detection updated and running, scan your computer weekly, watch Task Manager for unexpected programs

plainName: Attacker Runs Commands on Your Computer
realWorldScenario: Attacker gains access and runs commands on your computer or server - like an IT person would use Command Prompt or Terminal. They can do anything: install malware, steal files, delete data, or move laterally to other computers.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual system activity, processes running with uncommon commands, files being modified unexpectedly
singleActionToTake: Run EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) software that monitors for suspicious commands, restrict who can run commands on critical systems

plainName: Attacker Hijacks Software Libraries Your Programs Depend On
realWorldScenario: Your software depends on smaller libraries to work. Attacker replaces these libraries with malicious versions that do what the original ones did PLUS install malware. You use the program normally but malware installs silently.
targetedTypes: Software developers, software companies
howYouWouldKnow: Programs behave unexpectedly, new permissions being requested, unexplained network activity from programs
singleActionToTake: Keep all software updated to latest versions, verify you're downloading software from official sources, use supply chain security tools
industryNotes: Software supply chain attacks are increasing - SolarWinds, npm, PyPI have been targeted

plainName: Attacker Gathers Files Together Before Stealing Them
realWorldScenario: Attacker collects valuable files from various locations on your network into one place, then steals them all together. This makes it faster and easier to steal data.
targetedTypes: Any organization with valuable data
howYouWouldKnow: Files being moved to unusual locations, large folder created with mixed file types, data being packaged together
singleActionToTake: Monitor for suspicious file movement, use data loss prevention (DLP) tools that alert when large amounts of data are being collected

plainName: Attacker Steals Windows Passwords From Computer Memory
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses specialized tools to extract password hashes or credentials from a Windows computer's memory. These passwords can then be cracked or used to access other systems.
targetedTypes: Windows organizations, especially those with shared admin credentials
howYouWouldKnow: You won't know immediately - but you might notice unauthorized access from stolen credentials
singleActionToTake: Use unique passwords for each account, enable MFA, disable Password Save features, use credential guard on Windows
industryNotes: Tools like Mimikatz make credential theft easy

plainName: Attacker Discovers Your Organization's Network Structure
realWorldScenario: Once inside your network, attacker finds out how everything is connected - what computers exist, which servers are important, how departments connect. This information helps them find valuable targets.
targetedTypes: Medium to large organizations with multiple networks/departments
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual network scanning activity, repeated DNS lookups for unusual addresses
singleActionToTake: Monitor network traffic, use firewalls to restrict which systems can talk to each other, segment your network so not everything can reach everything

plainName: Attacker Gathers Information About Your Operating System and Software
realWorldScenario: Attacker learns what operating system you use, what version, what patches you have (or don't have), what software is installed. This helps them find vulnerabilities they can exploit.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: You won't know this is happening - it's passive information gathering
singleActionToTake: Keep all software and operating systems patched and updated immediately when updates are released

plainName: Attacker Explores Your Folders and Files
realWorldScenario: Attacker browses through folders and files on your computer or network to understand what you have, find valuable data, and look for more passwords or clues for further attacks.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual folder access patterns, files being read that nobody normally touches
singleActionToTake: Limit folder permissions - employees should only access folders they need for their job, monitor file access

plainName: Attacker Listens to Network Traffic to Steal Information
realWorldScenario: Attacker on your network captures network traffic (data being sent between computers) and looks for passwords, credit cards, confidential messages, or other valuable information that isn't encrypted.
targetedTypes: Organizations with unencrypted network traffic
howYouWouldKnow: Passwords being compromised, unusual network slowness, attacker having information they shouldn't know
singleActionToTake: Use HTTPS and encrypted connections for everything, encrypt all passwords and sensitive data in transit, use VPNs
industryNotes: This is why public WiFi is dangerous - anyone can listen

plainName: Attacker Learns About Your Employees and Organization
realWorldScenario: Attacker researches your employees on LinkedIn, social media, company website, and other public sources to find names, titles, relationships, and other information useful for targeted phishing attacks.
targetedTypes: Any organization with employees who have social media presence
howYouWouldKnow: Phishing emails that seem to know details about your organization or role
singleActionToTake: Train employees on phishing awareness, limit personal information on social media, company directory should be private

plainName: Attacker Sends Test Phishing Emails to Find Valid Targets
realWorldScenario: Before launching a full attack, attacker sends test phishing emails to see which ones get through, which employees click, and which addresses are valid. This helps them refine their attack.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: Suspicious emails asking for verification, requests that look slightly off, unusual sender addresses
singleActionToTake: Use email filtering, educate employees on phishing, report suspicious emails to IT, consider security awareness training

plainName: Attacker Compromises Software or Services You Buy and Use
realWorldScenario: Instead of attacking you directly, attacker compromises a software vendor or cloud service you rely on. When you download updates or use the service, malware is delivered to you.
targetedTypes: Any organization using commercial software - SolarWinds incident affected 18,000+ organizations
howYouWouldKnow: Unexpected behavior from trusted software, security advisories from vendors, antivirus alerts on legitimate software
singleActionToTake: Keep all software updated, monitor vendor security announcements, verify software authenticity, use reputable vendors
industryNotes: SolarWinds, Kaseya, and 3CX supply chain attacks proved this is a major threat

plainName: Attacker Buys Infrastructure to Carry Out Attack
realWorldScenario: Attacker registers fake domains that look like your company, rents servers, buys hosting - all the infrastructure needed to run a phishing campaign or host malware. They do this anonymously before attacking.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: You won't know until the attack happens
singleActionToTake: Monitor for fake domains using your company name, work with security vendors on threat intelligence, set up domain alerts

plainName: Attacker Tricks Someone Into Clicking or Opening Malware
realWorldScenario: Attacker relies on you or your employees to make a mistake - clicking a link, opening an attachment, downloading a file, entering credentials on a fake website. The attack only works because humans trust and make mistakes.
targetedTypes: Everyone
howYouWouldKnow: Malware on your computer, credential theft, unauthorized access to accounts
singleActionToTake: Be suspicious of unexpected emails and links, never enter passwords on websites you haven't typed the URL for, educate yourself and employees

plainName: Attacker Uses Your Trusted Business Partners to Get In
realWorldScenario: Attacker compromises a vendor, contractor, or partner organization that has access to your network. They then use that trusted connection to get into YOUR network, knowing you'll trust traffic from them.
targetedTypes: Large organizations with many vendors and partners
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual activity from partner networks, credentials from partners being misused
singleActionToTake: Manage third-party access carefully, verify partners' security practices, limit what partners can access on your network, monitor partner connections

plainName: Attacker Remotely Executes Commands on Your Windows Computers
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) - a legitimate Windows feature - to run commands on your computers remotely. This is like an IT person using management tools, but maliciously.
targetedTypes: Windows-based organizations without proper access controls
howYouWouldKnow: Unexpected WMI activity in logs, processes starting that you didn't authorize, lateral movement on network
singleActionToTake: Restrict WMI access, monitor WMI activity, limit local admin privileges, use EDR tools

plainName: Attacker Lateral ly Moves Through Your Network
realWorldScenario: Attacker gets access to one computer, then uses it to hop to other computers in your network. They move from computer to computer looking for higher-value targets, sensitive data, or administrative access.
targetedTypes: Medium to large organizations with multiple connected computers
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual account activity across multiple systems, impossible travel (same account active in multiple locations), network scanning activity
singleActionToTake: Segment your network so not everything can reach everything, use unique credentials for admin accounts, monitor for unusual lateral movement

plainName: Attacker Monitors What You Do (Keylogging, Screen Recording)
realWorldScenario: Attacker installs software that records everything you type (keylogging) or takes screenshots of your screen. This captures passwords, emails, confidential conversations, anything you do on the computer.
targetedTypes: Any organization - used against executives and high-value targets
howYouWouldKnow: Computer slower than usual, unusual disk or network activity, antivirus alerts, passwords being compromised
singleActionToTake: Keep antivirus updated, don't download unknown software, monitor running processes, use hardware security keys instead of passwords when possible

plainName: Attacker Uses Legitimate Remote Access Tools to Get In
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses tools that companies normally use for legitimate purposes (Remote Desktop, TeamViewer, AnyDesk, SSH) to gain access. Since these tools are trusted, they can move through your network without being detected.
targetedTypes: Any organization with remote access capabilities
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual remote connections, remote access from unexpected locations, sessions running when nobody is there
singleActionToTake: Require MFA for all remote access, monitor remote access attempts, disable unnecessary remote access protocols, restrict who can use remote tools

plainName: Attacker Moves Between Computers Over Network
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses various methods to move from one computer on your network to another - Remote Desktop, SSH, WMI, or other network protocols. Each move gets them closer to their target.
targetedTypes: Any networked organization
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual network connections between systems, unexpected file shares being accessed, lateral movement indicators
singleActionToTake: Monitor and restrict network connections, use network segmentation, require MFA for critical systems, monitor for lateral movement

plainName: Attacker Creates Scheduled Tasks to Run Malware Later
realWorldScenario: Attacker installs malware as a scheduled task (like Windows Task Scheduler on Linux cron). This runs automatically at certain times or intervals, giving them persistent access and ability to cause damage on a schedule.
targetedTypes: Any organization
howYouWouldKnow: Unexpected scheduled tasks, malware running at specific times, unusual activity on regular schedule
singleActionToTake: Review scheduled tasks regularly, restrict who can create tasks, monitor for suspicious task creation, use EDR tools

plainName: Attacker Uses Legitimate Windows Programs to Hide Malware
realWorldScenario: Attacker abuses legitimate Windows programs (PowerShell, WMI, Registry, Outlook, Windows Update) to hide and run malware. Since these programs are trusted, security tools don't notice the malware.
targetedTypes: Windows organizations
howYouWouldKnow: Legitimate programs behaving unusually, unexpected activity from normal programs, antivirus alerts from trusted programs
singleActionToTake: Monitor how legitimate programs are being used, restrict script execution, use application whitelisting, run EDR
industryNotes: Living off the land' attacks are increasingly common

plainName: Attacker Uses Stolen Credentials to Access Your Cloud Accounts
realWorldScenario: Attacker has stolen credentials or session tokens and uses them to log into your cloud accounts (Office 365, AWS, Google Workspace, etc) as if they were a legitimate user. They appear to come from normal places.
targetedTypes: Any organization using cloud services
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual cloud activity, data being accessed or copied, email forwarding rules created, unexpected changes to cloud settings
singleActionToTake: Enable MFA on all cloud accounts, monitor for unusual access, review session activity regularly, set up alerts for critical changes

plainName: Attacker Covers Their Tracks by Deleting Logs
realWorldScenario: Attacker clears log files and erases evidence of their activities so investigators can't see what they did or how they got in. This is like erasing security camera footage after committing a crime.
targetedTypes: Any organization - often done before final attack
howYouWouldKnow: Gaps in log files, 'log cleared' messages, missing data that should be there, time gaps in historical records
singleActionToTake: Send logs to external storage/service so attacker can't delete them, use write-once storage for logs, monitor for log deletion attempts

plainName: Attacker Changes Your Websites or Applications
realWorldScenario: Attacker modifies your public website or application - changes the homepage, inserts messages, or installs malware that affects your customers. This damages your reputation and can trick customers into providing information.
targetedTypes: Organizations with web presence
howYouWouldKnow: Website looks different, malware warnings from browsers, customers reporting fake content, website unavailable
singleActionToTake: Monitor website for unauthorized changes, restrict who can modify website, use web firewalls, keep CMS software updated

plainName: Attacker Erases Your Hard Drive or Makes Data Inaccessible
realWorldScenario: Attacker deliberately destroys data - wipes hard drives, deletes databases, corrupts files - to cause damage. Different from ransomware because they're not asking for money, just causing destruction.
targetedTypes: Organizations with valuable data or critical infrastructure
howYouWouldKnow: Data suddenly unavailable, hard drives showing as empty, database corruption, system won't boot
singleActionToTake: Back up everything regularly to external storage/cloud, test restores to ensure backups work, have disaster recovery plan

plainName: Attacker Deletes or Corrupts Your Backups
realWorldScenario: Attacker doesn't just encrypt your files with ransomware - they also delete your backups so you can't recover. This forces you to either pay the ransom or lose everything.
targetedTypes: Organizations with inadequate backup protection
howYouWouldKnow: Backups missing, corrupted backup files, unable to restore from backup, backup storage encrypted
singleActionToTake: Keep backups offline or in separate cloud account, test backup restores monthly, use immutable backups, have multiple backup copies

plainName: Attacker Overwrites Your Hard Drive Completely
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses specialized tools to completely overwrite all data on your hard drive(s), making them completely unrecoverable. This is permanent and catastrophic data destruction.
targetedTypes: Organizations in critical infrastructure or conflict zones
howYouWouldKnow: Computer won't boot, all data gone, hard drive shows as new/empty
singleActionToTake: Maintain offline backups not connected to network, have disaster recovery plan, test recovery procedures
industryNotes: Seen in destructive attacks like NotPetya, WhisperGate

plainName: Attacker Exfiltrates Your Data to Sell or Leak It
realWorldScenario: Attacker steals your confidential data and either sells it to others, leaks it publicly, or uses it for extortion. This includes customer data, financial records, intellectual property, or trade secrets.
targetedTypes: Any organization with valuable data
howYouWouldKnow: Data appearing publicly, ransom demands, customers notified of data breach, regulatory notifications required
singleActionToTake: Encrypt sensitive data, limit who can access valuable data, monitor for unusual data transfers, have incident response plan
industryNotes: Ransomware gangs now use 'double extortion' - encrypt AND threaten to sell data

plainName: Attacker Uses Legitimate Cloud Services for Malware Command & Control
realWorldScenario: Attacker uses cloud services you trust (Slack, Discord, Telegram, cloud storage) as the 'control center' for their malware. Malware receives commands through these services, making it harder to detect.
targetedTypes: Any organization using cloud services
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual activity on cloud services, computers connecting to unusual cloud accounts, network restrictions not blocking legitimate services
singleActionToTake: Monitor cloud service traffic, restrict what cloud services employees can use at work, use proxy/firewall to monitor activity

plainName: Attacker Compromises Updates From Software Vendors
realWorldScenario: Attacker breaks into a software vendor's update system and inserts malware into software updates. When you install the 'legitimate' update, you're actually installing malware that the vendor provided without knowing.
targetedTypes: Large organizations that get targeted by sophisticated attackers
howYouWouldKnow: Security advisories from vendors, antivirus alerts on update files, vendor security breaches reported in news
singleActionToTake: Monitor vendor security announcements, verify software authenticity, consider delaying updates briefly to see if issues are reported
industryNotes: SolarWinds supply chain attack affected 18,000 organizations worldwide

plainName: Attacker Adds Legitimate-Looking Additional Credentials to Accounts
realWorldScenario: Attacker creates additional login credentials (passwords, API keys, SSH keys) for existing user accounts. This provides permanent backdoor access - even if the original user changes their password, attacker still has access.
targetedTypes: Organizations with privileged accounts
howYouWouldKnow: Account activity from unexpected locations, unexpected API activity, SSH key authentication logs you don't recognize
singleActionToTake: Review all authentication methods for accounts regularly, delete unused SSH keys/API tokens, implement conditional access rules

plainName: Attacker Creates Additional Domain Admin Accounts
realWorldScenario: Attacker adds new domain admin accounts to your Active Directory - accounts that look legitimate but are controlled by the attacker. This gives them permanent administrative access to your entire network.
targetedTypes: Medium to large organizations with Active Directory
howYouWouldKnow: New admin accounts you don't recognize, unusual admin activity, domain admin activity from unexpected locations
singleActionToTake: Regularly audit all domain admin accounts, monitor domain admin activity closely, restrict who can create admin accounts, use Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs)

plainName: Attacker Creates Additional Cloud Account Admin Credentials
realWorldScenario: Attacker adds additional admin credentials to your cloud accounts (Office 365, AWS, Google Workspace) creating permanent backdoor access to your cloud infrastructure.
targetedTypes: Any organization using cloud services
howYouWouldKnow: New cloud admin accounts, unusual cloud admin activity, unexpected permission changes in cloud
singleActionToTake: Monitor cloud admin accounts closely, use conditional access policies, enable auditing for admin actions, use break-glass accounts with offline access only

plainName: Attacker Creates API Credentials For Persistent Access
realWorldScenario: Attacker generates API keys or tokens that allow programmatic access to your applications and data. These credentials can be used to steal data or manipulate systems without interactive login.
targetedTypes: Organizations with APIs and integrated applications
howYouWouldKnow: Unusual API activity, data being accessed via API, unexpected API token creation, API usage from odd IP addresses
singleActionToTake: Rotate API keys regularly, delete unused API keys, monitor API usage, implement rate limiting on APIs
